exemplar.title: How does the rate of cooling depend on the thickness of air trapped in a double glazing setup?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the rate of cooling depend on the thickness of air trapped in a double glazing setup?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are communicated with clarity and precision (units, headings, error bars, trend lines).
Raw and processed data are wellâformatted, facilitating comparison across trials.
Uncertainty propagation for ln(TâTa) in trial 1 is correctly executed and displayed.
Derivation and linearisation of Newtonâs law are presented clearly, supporting the analysis.
weaknesses: Uncertainty propagation is only applied to the first trial; later trials use a simplified maxâmin/2 method.
Ambientâtemperature variations are acknowledged but not incorporated quantitatively into uncertainties.
Samplingârate reporting is inconsistent (1 Hz vs. 1000 Hz).
Occasional truncated axis labels and an erroneous linearisation equation need correction.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing is both clear and precise, satisfying the top descriptor.  Consideration of uncertainties and processing quality are strong but contain significant omissions (incomplete propagation, inconsistent reporting), placing this criterion just below perfection at 5/6.

strengths: Research question situated in a very specific and appropriate realâworld context (cabin heat loss, glazing and energy efficiency).
Methodology description (apparatus list, diagrams, procedure) is sufficiently detailed to allow full reproduction.
Methodological considerations (controls, justifications for sample size and range, safety) are clearly explained and linked to data quality.
Clear, wellâlabelled schematic and variable tables enhance reproducibility.
Hypothesis diagram and risk assessment demonstrate thorough forethought.
weaknesses: Diagram lacks explicit dimensions (beaker heights, gap distances) for exact reconstruction.
Variable definitions (e.g., âxâ) are occasionally unclear and need correction.
No specific numerical range for airâgap thickness stated in the research question.
Radiation effects are omitted from controlledâvariables discussion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Research Design meet the topâband descriptors: the question is framed within an explicit, appropriate context; the methodology allows full reproduction; and methodological considerations are comprehensively explained.  Minor omissions (dimensions, variable definitions, quantitative range, radiation control) do not undermine overall excellence, warranting full marks (6/6).

strengths: Conclusion links the experimental optimum to plotted data and processed Gaussian fit.
Literature comparison is wellâexecuted, citing peerâreviewed sources and contextualizing results against accepted ranges.
Justified comparison to established scientific context demonstrates a strong understanding of relevance.
weaknesses: Choice of a Gaussian fit lacks physical justification from heatâtransfer theory.
Broad uncertainty and sparseness of data points weaken the conclusionâs full consistency with analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is directly supported by the analysis and compared to accepted scientific context, meeting the topâband descriptor for context and justification.  Minor modelâchoice concerns and wide uncertainties prevent awarding a perfect 6, resulting in 5/6.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified (wallâthickness variance, ambient fluctuations, Newtonâs law assumptions).
Realistic and relevant improvements are proposed (matching wall thickness, centring guides, extended range, foam lids).
Critical recognition of Newtonâs law limitations shows thoughtful evaluation.
weaknesses: Explanations of how each improvement quantitatively reduces uncertainty are brief and not systematically analysed.
Relative impact of specific weaknesses is discussed qualitatively but not explained in depth or ranked by significance.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student describes specific methodological limitations and realistic improvements, fulfilling the midâband descriptors.  While the weaknesses are identified and improvements suggested, the lack of systematic, quantitative explanation of their relative impact places this work at 4/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly articulated (âHow does the rate of cooling depend on the thickness of air trapped in a double-glazing set-up?â) and is framed within the clearly described real-world context of cold cabins, domestic glazing and energy efficiency.  The student links the investigation to practical applications, showing why the question matters and defining the specific physical situation being modelled.  This reflects a specific and appropriate context rather than a broad or generic one, satisfying the top-band descriptor; a minor lack of reference to quantitative ranges used in industry prevents awarding the full 6.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted, propagated through the ln transformation, and displayed as error bars.  However, later averaging uses only max-min/2 without propagating trial-by-trial uncertainties, and ambient-temperature variation is acknowledged but not incorporated.  Thus consideration is adequate but contains significant omissions.

The student explains why each controlled/uncontrolled variable matters (e.g., convection through lids, wall thickness) and how it is managed.  Justifications for sample size, range and safety considerations are also supplied.  While some controls (ambient temperature stability) are not fully achieved, the treatment moves beyond a simple list and provides reasoned links to data quality, meeting the âexplainedâ standard.

Specific weaknessesâe.g., beaker wall thickness variance, ambient fluctuations, imperfect lids, Newton-law assumptionâare described and their potential influence on cooling rate qualitatively discussed.  The relative impact (which error dominates) is hinted at but not systematically analysed, aligning with âdescribesâ rather than fully âexplainsâ, hence upper mid-band.

Apparatus lists, diagrams, tables of variables and a numbered procedure (steps 1-17) allow another investigator to reproduce the data-collection process with only minimal interpretation.  Key specifications (beaker diameters, sampling rate, sensor accuracy, repetitions, distance measurement method) are given, and controls for convection and conduction are described.  Some ambiguity remains over exact positioning of lids and centring of beakers, hence a 5 rather than 6.

Processing (linearisation, gradient extraction, averaging rates, Gaussian fit) is largely appropriate and correctly executed, but the decision to discard full uncertainty treatment for later trials and the questionable suitability of a Gaussian model introduce noticeable inconsistency.  Hence âsome significant inaccuraciesâ place it in the 3-4 band, at the upper end.

For each stated weakness the student proposes a realistic, relevant improvement (matching beaker wall thickness, conducting all trials in one session, precise centring guides, foam lids, extended distance range).  While sensible, the explanations of how each improvement would specifically mitigate error are brief, so the work reaches the âdescribedâ standard (3-4) at its top end.

Data tables include units, correct headings and uncertainties; graphs are labelled with scales, trend lines and error bars.  Linearised plots and processed-data graphs are neat and readable.  Minor lapses (occasional truncated axis labels) are negligible, so communication is both clear and precise.

Two peer-reviewed sources are quoted, giving accepted optimal separations (20-30 mm and 20-25 mm).  The student compares these ranges directly with the experimental result and notes agreement, providing a justified linkage to established context.  This meets the descriptor for the top band; limited depth of discussion justifies 5 not 6.

The student concludes that the optimal gap is 25 Â± 10 mm, a value derived from the processed Gaussian fit and clearly linked to the plotted data.  Yet the broad uncertainty, sparse data points and acknowledgement that Newtonâs law may not apply fully mean the conclusion is only partly secure.  It is therefore âdescribed but not fully consistentâ, meriting the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively situates the research question in a realâworld context (cabin heat loss, glazing and energy efficiency), showing clear relevance and motivating the investigation.
You note limitations of Newtonâs law (constant ambient temperature, small ÎT) but donât discuss how ambient fluctuations in your lab affect k. Consider quantifying ambient variability.
Table 1 clearly lists independent and dependent variables with apparatus and uncertainties, enabling reproducibility.
Controlled variables table omits radiation effects. Thermal radiation can contribute to heat loss; include a method to minimize or quantify it.
In the uncontrolled variables table you list glass thickness variance but do not quantify its impact. Measure wall thickness variation and incorporate it into uncertainty estimates.
The controlled variables caption (Table 2) correctly labels variables, significance, and control methods.
Hypothesis diagram is wellâconceptualized, combining inverseâthickness and convective effects to predict an optimal minimum.
Apparatus list repeats earlier items and misses Logger Pro calibration details. Consolidate lists and include sensor calibration procedure.
In the background you state âx is the temperature differenceâ in equation (1), but x represents the thickness of the insulating layer. Clarify variable definitions for reproducibility.
The research question is clearly stated but omits the specific range of airâgap thicknesses to be investigated. Consider specifying the numerical range to focus the context.
The discussion of convection effects is qualitative; you could strengthen the background by citing a quantitative source on convection onset in gas layers.
The derivation of Newtonâs law is presented clearly with correct integration to equation (3), demonstrating sound understanding of the exponential cooling model.
Equation (4) erroneously linearises as ââk ln(t)â instead of ââk tâ. The slope corresponds to âk, not âk ln(t). Correct this to ensure accurate data analysis.
Only a 20 s subset of raw data is shown without justification. Explain why these initial points represent the cooling trend or provide the complete dataset.
The average rate uses maxâmin/2 for uncertainty, ignoring propagation from individual trial errors. Consider weighted averaging with full uncertainty propagation.
Selecting a Gaussian fit for finding the optimum lacks physical justification. A model based on heat transfer theory would better represent expected behavior.
Literature comparison is wellâexecuted, citing peerâreviewed sources and linking the experimental optimum to published ranges (20â30 mm and 20â25 mm).
Improvement suggestions are realistic (beaker wall thickness matching, centring guides) but often lack explicit explanation of how they quantitatively reduce uncertainty.
Step 2 states sampling at 1 Hz for 600 s, but earlier you mention Logger Pro at 1000 Hz. Ensure consistent sampling rate reporting to avoid confusion.
The experimental setup schematic (Figure 2) is clear and wellâlabelled, aiding reproducibility of apparatus arrangement.
The setup diagram lacks explicit dimensions (beaker heights, gap distances). Add scale annotations to ensure exact reconstruction.
The risk assessment table covers safety and environmental considerations comprehensively.
The raw data table is wellâformatted with units and uncertainties, facilitating clear data recording.
Provide justification for truncating the yâaxis to 68â80 Â°C in the temperatureâtime graph or supply an inset showing the full scale.
The sample linearisation calculation is clear, but note that the slope sign will be negative for cooling data; discuss this to avoid misinterpretation.
Uncertainty propagation for ln (TâT_a) is correctly executed for trial 1, demonstrating sound handling of error analysis.
Only propagating uncertainty for the first trial leaves later trials without error bars. Apply the same propagation to each to maintain consistency.
The linearised graph is plotted with error bars, allowing a precise determination of k; this enhances the clarity of processing.
You observe curvature in the linearised plot and correctly identify Newtonâs law limitations; this insight demonstrates critical evaluation of data processing.
Processed data table is comprehensive, showing all trials, averages, and uncertainties for each gap size, facilitating comparison.
The discussion of Newtonâs law limitations reflects thoughtful recognition of incorrect theoretical assumptions and their impact on results.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA
